description: If A implies B, then the falsity of B implies the falsity of A.

hints.text: This is the **contrapositive**: if $A$ implies $B$, then $\lnot B$ implies $\lnot A$. (If $B$ is impossible, then $A$ must not hold either — otherwise $A$ would force $B$.) You'll need `not-intro` to prove $\lnot A$. Start by assuming $A \to B$.
Now you're proving $\lnot B \to \lnot A$. As always with implications, assume the antecedent: $\lnot B$.
To prove $\lnot A$ using `not-intro`, you need two subproofs sharing $A$ — one proving some $p$, the other proving $\lnot p$. For $p$, choose $B$. First subproof: assume $A$.
From $A \to B$ (step 1) and your fresh $A$ (step 3), use modus ponens to get $B$. This is your positive subproof — context contains $A$, conclusion is $B$.
Second subproof: you need a step whose context contains $A$ and whose conclusion is $\lnot B$. Merge $A$ (step 3) with $\lnot B$ (step 2) using `and-intro`.
Extract $\lnot B$ from the merged conjunction with `and-elim-r`. Now its context contains $A$.
Steps 4 and 6 both have $A$ in context and prove $B$ and $\lnot B$ — the contradiction. Apply `not-intro A`.
Discharge $\lnot B$ to get $\lnot B \to \lnot A$.
Discharge $A \to B$ to finish the contrapositive.
